title: Coevolution Arms Race
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Generation-by-generation simulation showing how reciprocal selection pressures drive trait escalation between predator and prey populations

learningObjectives: Predict how reciprocal selection pressures drive trait escalation in coevolving species
Explain why neither predator nor prey gains a lasting advantage in an evolutionary arms race
Analyze how mutation rate and selection strength affect the pace of coevolution
prerequisites: Understanding of natural selection and heritable traits

keyQuestions: Why do both predator and prey traits escalate over time?
What happens to the arms race when mutation rate increases?
Why does neither population gain a lasting advantage?
How does the Red Queen hypothesis explain coevolutionary arms races?
What are real-world examples of coevolution between predators and prey?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think evolution has a goal or endpoint, rather than understanding it as an ongoing process driven by selection pressures
Many students believe the predator should always win because it is stronger, not understanding that prey evolve counter-adaptations
Students may think coevolution only involves predator-prey relationships, not recognizing mutualistic coevolution
assessmentOpportunities: Students compare trait escalation rates under different mutation and selection parameters
Students explain the Red Queen hypothesis using evidence from their simulation runs
Students predict outcomes of introducing a third species and justify their reasoning
